2004-05-20‚Äé"this is it   Disgruntled-with-Tech-Support asks: ""Let's face it: At some point or another
column2: we've had to deal with some form of tech support. Quite often
column3: it's a hit-or-miss experience depending on the level of support required. Occasionally
column6: or nonsensical explanations result from the problems reported
column7: such as this one: I had just had DSL installed
column8: only to find it much slower than the 56K line I was looking to get rid of. On calling the provider
column9: I was told (by someone who likely reading off cue cards) to visit one of their internal websites for measuring bandwidth. While there
column10: I observed that they had both bytes per second and bits per second listed
column11: and that the number of bytes/sec != bits/sec * 8
column12: rather a factor around 13 or 14. I pointed this out as a possible problem

2004-06-16‚Äé": ""I'm tired of HD failures. I've suffered through a few of them. Even with backups
column2: they are still a pain to recover from. I've got all fairly inexpensive but reliable hardware picked out
column3: but I'm just not sure which RAID level to implement. My goals are to build a file server that can live through a drive failure with no loss of data
column4: and will be easy to rebuild. Ideally
column5: in the event of a failure
column6: I'd just like to remove the bad hard drive and install a new one and be done with it. Is this possible? How many drives to I need to get this done
column8: 4 or 5? What size should they be? I know when you implementddddd RAID
column9: your usable drive space is N% of the total drive space depending on the RAID level."""
